text:
Hello writers and book enthusiasts. Apologies for the extended absence—recent months have been unusually hectic. Fortunately, things are beginning to stabilize, and I'm excited to return to blogging and possibly more structured writing in the coming year.

As if 2020 itself were not extraordinary enough, I recently started thinking about how future authors will approach stories that are set partially or entirely during this year. The idea for this reflection wasn't originally mine; it came from a question posed by my husband, which quickly sent my thoughts in motion.

Earlier this week, I was reading The Invisible Life of Addie LaRue by V.E. Schwab—a novel I highly recommend. The story follows Addie, a woman who trades her soul for immortality, tracing her journey from 18th-century France to modern-day New York City in 2014. While explaining the premise to my husband, he asked when the book was set. That simple question evolved into a broader one: how will writers handle the year 2020 in fiction?

What role will 2020 ultimately play in literature? Will it become a defining moment, frequently referenced and explored, or will it be a period that writers deliberately avoid? Some novels—particularly in genres like romance—have already incorporated the pandemic as a central element, though I haven't explored those yet. It's intriguing to imagine speculative genres tackling this year: an urban fantasy where supernatural conflicts unfold in deserted cities, or alternate universes attributing COVID-19 to forces far beyond humanity.

Beyond fantasy, literary fiction may also grapple with the pandemic's emotional and societal weight. Will COVID-19 function merely as a plot device, or will writers imbue it with layered symbolic meaning? It's important to state clearly that this discussion does not minimize the severity of the pandemic or the devastating global loss of life it has caused.

From a narrative standpoint, 2020 presents logistical challenges. With much of the world confined indoors, traditional character movement and exploration are limited. If fictional characters choose to ignore these constraints, how will readers perceive them? Our real-world decisions during this time are heavily scrutinized, and that judgment will likely extend to fictional characters as well. These moral and social tensions, however, may provide fertile ground for meaningful discussion and compelling storytelling.

metadata.notes: English contemporary fiction story about writing during 2020
